title: The First Law vs Gentlemen Bastards: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Grimdark fantasy compared: Joe Abercrombie's First Law vs Scott Lynch's Gentlemen Bastards. Which dark fantasy series should you read first?

book1.title: The Blade Itself
book1.subtitle: The First Law, Book 1
book1.author: Joe Abercrombie
book1.description: Logen Ninefingers, infamous barbarian, has finally run out of luck. Caught in one feud too many, he's on the verge of becoming a dead barbarian—leaving nothing behind but bad songs, dead friends, and a lot of happy enemies. Nobleman Captain Jezal dan Luthar, dashing officer, and paragon of selfishness, has nothing more dangerous in mind than fleecing his friends at cards and dreaming of glory in the fencing circle. But war is brewing, and on the battlefields of the frozen North they fight by altogether bloodier rules.

book2.title: The Lies of Locke Lamora
book2.subtitle: Gentlemen Bastards, Book 1
book2.author: Scott Lynch
book2.description: An orphan's life is harsh—and often short—in the mysterious island city of Camorr. But young Locke Lamora dodges death and slavery, becoming a thief under the tutelage of a gifted con artist. As leader of the band of light-fingered brothers known as the Gentlemen Bastards, Locke is soon infamous, fooling even the underworld's most feared ruler. But in the shadows lurks someone still more ambitious and deadly. Faced with a bloody coup that threatens to destroy everyone and everything that holds meaning in his mercenary life, Locke vows to beat the enemy at his own brutal game—or die trying.

book1Strengths: 167,000 ratings at 4.4 stars—one of grimdark's foundational series alongside Malazan and Prince of Thorns. Published 2006, Abercrombie helped define the genre. Complete trilogy (The Blade Itself, Before They Are Hanged, Last Argument of Kings) plus standalones

Glokta the torturer is unforgettable—once a celebrated swordsman, now crippled and bitter, he tortures people for the Inquisition while hating himself. His internal monologue ('Body found floating by the docks') is darkly hilarious and deeply cynical. One of fantasy's most complex POVs

The 'no one is safe' factor is real—Abercrombie kills, maims, and breaks characters brutally. Heroic sacrifices backfire. Noble gestures get punished. Wars are won by the wrong people. If you're tired of plot armor, this delivers consequences

The dialogue is phenomenal—Abercrombie writes banter like Tarantino. Logen's 'You have to be realistic about these things,' Glokta's bitter sarcasm, Jezal's entitled whining. Every character has a distinct voice. The conversations are brutal, funny, and quotable

Subverts every fantasy trope—the barbarian hero is a mass murderer trying to reform, the noble officer is a selfish prick, the wizard is manipulative and amoral, the rebellion fails, the evil empire wins. This is anti-Tolkien fantasy. No good guys, just people making bad choices

The ending is perfect cynical commentary—all the characters' struggles change nothing. The powerful stay powerful, the idealistic get crushed, and the cycle continues. Some readers hate this, but it's thematically brilliant. 'The blade itself incites to deeds of violence'
book1Weaknesses: The Blade Itself is SLOW—the first book is 531 pages of character setup and world-building with minimal plot movement. The payoff comes in books 2-3, but many readers bounce off book 1. You need patience for Abercrombie's long game

Relentlessly cynical and bleak—there are no heroes, no happy endings, no hope that good prevails. If you need characters to root for or believe change is possible, this will depress you. It's grimdark nihilism taken to the extreme

Graphic violence and torture—Glokta's chapters include detailed torture scenes (pulling teeth, breaking fingers, worse). Battle scenes are visceral and brutal. Abercrombie doesn't flinch from the ugliness of violence. Not for sensitive readers

The 'nothing changes' theme can feel pointless—after three books of struggle, the world is basically the same. The powerful manipulate everyone, the idealistic get crushed, cycles repeat. Thematically coherent but emotionally unsatisfying for readers wanting progress or justice
book2Strengths: 234,000 ratings at 4.5 stars—MORE popular than First Law despite being incomplete (3 of 7 books published). The Lies of Locke Lamora (2006) is a phenomenon. People love this book passionately

Locke Lamora is fantasy's most charming con artist—orphan thief who runs elaborate long cons on the rich nobility of Camorr. 'Nice bird, asshole' is his defining moment. He's witty, clever, loyal to his crew, and utterly captivating. You WANT him to succeed

The Gentlemen Bastards crew is perfect—Locke and Jean's friendship is the heart of the series. The banter between the crew (Bug, Calo, Galdo) is hilarious and touching. When tragedy hits, it devastates because you love these characters

It's Ocean's Eleven meets fantasy—elaborate heists, clever cons, witty banter, everything goes wrong, improvised recovery. If you loved Ocean's 11-13, this is your fantasy book. The Secret Peace of Camorr con is brilliant

Camorr (Venice-inspired) is gorgeously rendered—canals, Elderglass towers (indestructible ancient structures), the Floating Grave, the Five Towers. Lynch's worldbuilding is immersive and specific. You can SEE this city

Dual timeline structure works brilliantly—present-day heist intercut with flashbacks showing how Locke became who he is. The flashbacks deepen character and inform present action. Lynch uses this structure masterfully
book2Weaknesses: Series is INCOMPLETE—only 3 of 7 books published. Book 1 (2006), Book 2 (2007), Book 3 (2013), then 13-year gap. Book 4 is 'in progress' indefinitely. Scott Lynch has struggled with depression and writing blocks. You might die before this series finishes

Red Seas Under Red Skies (book 2) is significantly weaker—switches from heist to pirate adventure, loses the Camorr setting, feels like a detour. Many readers rank it their least favorite. Book 3 (Republic of Thieves) returns to form but the series is inconsistent

The violence is BRUTAL despite the fun tone—Lynch doesn't pull punches. The Gray King plotline includes horrific torture and violence. Characters you love die horribly. The whiplash from witty banter to graphic violence is jarring
No resolution for a decade+—Book 3 ended on major cliffhangers in 2013. Still waiting. If you need closure or hate incomplete series, this will torture you. First Law trilogy is DONE

book1: You have to be realistic about these things.
The blade itself incites to deeds of violence.
Once you've got a task to do, it's better to do it than live with the fear of it.
We should forgive our enemies, but not before they are hanged.
Body found floating by the docks... bloated and crawling with flies. Know him?
book2: I only steal because my dear old family needs the money to live!
Someday, Locke Lamora, someday, you're going to f*** up so magnificently, so grandiosely, so epically, that when they write about it, you'll be dead.
Difficult and impossible are cousins often mistaken for one another, with very little in common.
A sellsword is someone who is paid money to kill people. There's no delicate way to define it.

book1: You want morally gray, grimdark fantasy that subverts heroic tropes
You love complex characters who aren't purely good or evil
You appreciate cynical humor and brutal realism
You want complete trilogy you can finish
You're ready for dark, violent fantasy with no safety net
book2: You want fantasy heist/con artist story—Ocean's Eleven meets Lies
You love witty banter and charming rogues
You prefer fun, entertaining fantasy over pure grimdark
You want Venice-inspired setting with gorgeous world-building
You don't mind waiting for incomplete series (3 of 7 books)

verdict.summary: The Lies of Locke Lamora wins for immediate entertainment with 234,000 ratings at 4.5 stars—Ocean's Eleven meets fantasy, charming con artist protagonist, Gentlemen Bastards crew banter, Venice-inspired Camorr, dual timeline structure. First Law wins for complete grimdark trilogy with 167,000 at 4.4 stars—Glokta the torturer's POV, subverts all fantasy tropes, Tarantino-level dialogue, 'no one is safe' consequences, perfect cynical ending. WARNING: Locke Lamora series incomplete (3 of 7 books, 13-year gap). First Law complete. For fun, choose Locke. For bleakness and completion, choose First Law.
verdict.recommendation: Read The Lies of Locke Lamora first if you want Ocean's Eleven meets fantasy—brilliant heist plot, Locke's charming con artistry ('Nice bird, asshole'), Gentlemen Bastards crew friendship (Locke/Jean banter), Venice-inspired Camorr (Elderglass towers, canals, Floating Grave), dual timeline flashbacks. It's FUN, witty, and emotionally engaging. BUT the series is incomplete (3 of 7 books, 13-year gap since book 3). Read The First Law if you want complete grimdark trilogy—Glokta the torturer's darkly hilarious POV ('Body found floating by the docks'), Tarantino-level dialogue, subverts every fantasy trope (barbarian is mass murderer, noble is selfish prick, rebellion fails), brutal consequences, perfect cynical ending where nothing changes. SLOW first book (531 pages setup) but payoff in books 2-3. WARNING: First Law is relentlessly bleak, no heroes, graphic torture/violence. Choose based on priorities—Locke for fun and charm (despite incompleteness), First Law for grimdark masterpiece (complete but soul-crushing). Both published 2006, both defined their subgenres.
